events.text: The patient is a 52-year-old female named Margaret Delacroix. She is of French-Canadian descent, non-smoker, with a significant family history of breast and ovarian cancer (mother died of ovarian cancer at age 58, maternal aunt had breast cancer at age 44). She works as a high school librarian and lives with her husband. She has two adult children. No prior malignancies. Menopausal status: postmenopausal (natural menopause at age 50).

At age 52 years, the patient had a diagnosis of FIGO Stage IIIC (AJCC pT3c pN1 M0) HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA OF THE OVARY (ICD-O-3 site code C56.9, histology code 8461/3: Serous cystadenocarcinoma, NOS). Diagnosis confirmed by positive histology from bilateral ovarian masses with peritoneal implants. Primary site: bilateral ovaries. Tumor size: right ovary 8.2 cm, left ovary 5.6 cm. Relevant features: ascites present (approximately 2.5 liters on imaging), peritoneal carcinomatosis with implants on the diaphragm, omentum, pelvic peritoneum, and paracolic gutters. Retroperitoneal lymph node involvement (pelvic and para-aortic). Estrogen receptor status: positive. Progesterone receptor: negative. CA-125 at diagnosis: 1,847 U/mL. HE4: 312 pmol/L. ROMA score: elevated (high risk). Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY from diagnostic laparoscopy prior to definitive surgery.

At age 52, the patient had a CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (staging CT), which showed cancer. Findings: bilateral complex ovarian masses (right 8.2 cm, left 5.6 cm) with solid and cystic components, thick septations, and papillary projections. Large volume ascites. Extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis with omental caking (omental cake measuring 12 x 8 cm), diaphragmatic implants bilaterally (right greater than left, up to 1.8 cm), pelvic peritoneal implants, and implants along the paracolic gutters. Enlarged pelvic lymph nodes (right external iliac 1.4 cm, left internal iliac 1.2 cm). Para-aortic lymph nodes up to 1.1 cm. No evidence of pleural effusion. Liver surface implants noted (largest 0.9 cm). No parenchymal liver or lung metastases. Impression: Findings consistent with advanced ovarian malignancy with peritoneal carcinomatosis, FIGO stage IIIC. No evidence of FIGO stage IV disease.

At age 52, the patient had a pathology result from diagnostic laparoscopy with peritoneal biopsy and omental biopsy. Specimen type: peritoneal implant biopsy (right pelvic peritoneum) and omental core biopsy. Gross description: multiple gray-white firm tissue fragments. Microscopic findings: high-grade serous carcinoma with characteristic slit-like spaces, papillary architecture, psammoma bodies, and marked nuclear atypia with prominent nucleoli. Mitotic rate high (>10 per 10 HPF). Immunohistochemistry: WT1 positive (diffuse, strong), PAX8 positive, p53 aberrant (overexpression pattern, diffuse strong positivity, consistent with TP53 missense mutation), CK7 positive, CK20 negative, ER positive (30%), PR negative. Ki-67 proliferative index: 75%. Diagnosis: HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, consistent with primary ovarian/fallopian tube origin. Note: Morphology and IHC profile support germline BRCA1 testing and molecular profiling.

At age 52 years, the patient had next generation sequencing (germline and somatic) performed for high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the peritoneal biopsy (omentum) and peripheral blood (germline). Germline findings: BRCA1 pathogenic variant c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756Profs*74), also known as 5382insC, a founder mutation prevalent in Ashkenazi Jewish and Eastern European populations - classified as PATHOGENIC per ACMG/AMP criteria. Somatic findings from tumor: TP53 p.R248W (VAF 68%, pathogenic, consistent with homozygous loss in tumor); BRCA1 somatic loss of heterozygosity (LOH) at the BRCA1 locus confirmed (consistent with biallelic inactivation). Additional somatic alterations: RB1 p.R358* (VAF 22%); CCNE1 amplification (copy number 8.2); MYC gain (copy number 4.1); KRAS wild-type; PIK3CA wild-type; CDK12 wild-type. Homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) score: 56 (above threshold of 42, HRD-positive). Genomic instability score: high. Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 4.2 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). PD-L1 expression: 5% TPS. Actionable alterations: BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant (PARP inhibitor sensitive); HRD-positive tumor. No NTRK fusions detected. No ALK or RET rearrangements.

At age 52, the patient had a gynecologic oncology consultation note. History and physical: 52-year-old postmenopausal woman presenting with 3-month history of progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, unintentional weight loss of 12 lbs, and pelvic pressure. Pelvic exam revealed bilateral adnexal masses. Performance status: ECOG 1. BMI: 26.4 kg/m2. No ascites-related respiratory compromise. CA-125 1,847 U/mL. Discussed diagnosis of stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian cancer with patient and husband. Disclosed germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC); genetic counseling referral placed. Family history notation: high-penetrance BRCA1 variant with implications for first-degree relatives. Treatment plan discussed: primary debulking surgery (PDS) with cytoreductive intent, targeting R0 resection, followed by platinum/taxane-based chemotherapy. Patient agreeable. Surgical risks discussed including bowel resection, colostomy, ureteral injury. Cancer present. No response data applicable at this initial visit.

At age 52 years, the patient underwent primary debulking surgery (exploratory laparotomy with cytoreductive surgery). Procedure: total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO), bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, stripping of diaphragmatic peritoneum bilaterally, resection of pelvic peritoneum, and excision of multiple peritoneal implants from paracolic gutters. Intraoperative findings: 2.2 liters of straw-colored ascites drained. Omental cake completely resected. Right ovarian mass 8 cm, left ovarian mass 6 cm, both arising from fallopian tubes. Multiple diaphragmatic implants stripped. Pelvic sidewall peritoneum resected. Liver surface implant (0.7 cm) excised. No bowel resection required. Estimated blood loss: 650 mL. 1 unit packed red blood cells transfused intraoperatively. Operative time: 4 hours 42 minutes. Cytoreduction result: R0 (no visible residual disease, complete gross resection). Pathologic confirmation of R0 pending final pathology.

At age 52, the patient had a surgical pathology report from primary debulking surgery specimens. Specimens submitted: right ovary/fallopian tube, left ovary/fallopian tube, uterus/cervix, omentum, appendix, pelvic peritoneum, diaphragmatic peritoneum (right and left), para-aortic lymph nodes (14 nodes), pelvic lymph nodes (22 nodes), liver surface implant. Findings: Right fallopian tube: high-grade serous carcinoma, tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC lesion) identified in the fimbriated end - consistent with fallopian tube origin. Right ovary: high-grade serous carcinoma involving ovarian cortex and surface, 8.1 cm. Left fallopian tube: STIC lesion present. Left ovary: high-grade serous carcinoma, 5.9 cm. Uterus: no carcinoma (leiomyomata incidentally noted). Omentum: high-grade serous carcinoma (omental cake, completely excised, 11.5 x 7.8 cm aggregate). Pelvic peritoneum: carcinoma present, margins negative. Diaphragmatic peritoneum: multiple foci of high-grade serous carcinoma, all excised. Liver surface: single focus of serous carcinoma, 0.6 cm, completely excised. Pelvic lymph nodes: 4/22 positive for metastatic carcinoma (largest deposit 1.3 cm). Para-aortic lymph nodes: 2/14 positive. Appendix: no carcinoma. Final pathologic stage: pT3c pN1 M0 (AJCC 8th edition). Cytoreduction: R0 confirmed - no macroscopic residual disease at completion of surgery.

At age 52, the patient had a post-operative visit at 4 weeks after primary debulking surgery. The patient is recovering well. Wound healing intact. No post-operative complications. Bowel function returned on post-operative day 3. Discharged on post-operative day 5. Current performance status ECOG 1. CA-125 post-operatively (day 21): 342 U/mL (significantly reduced from 1,847 U/mL pre-operatively, consistent with R0 resection). Discussed adjuvant chemotherapy plan: carboplatin AUC 6 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days for 6 cycles. Discussed BRCA1-related maintenance options following completion of chemotherapy (PARP inhibitor maintenance). Genetic counseling completed - cascade testing recommended for first-degree relatives. Cancer present (residual microscopic disease expected). Responding to surgical debulking.

At age 52 years, the patient received curative-intent chemotherapy with carboplatin AUC 6 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days, cycle 1 of 6. Pre-medications: dexamethasone 20 mg IV, diphenhydramine 50 mg IV, famotidine 20 mg IV. Carboplatin dose calculated by Calvert formula (GFR estimated by CKD-EPI): carboplatin 640 mg IV over 30 minutes; paclitaxel 315 mg IV over 3 hours. Cycle 1 administered without complications. BSA 1.80 m2. Tolerated well. Anti-emetic prophylaxis: ondansetron and dexamethasone.

At age 52, during cycle 2 of carboplatin/paclitaxel, the patient developed Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (numbness and tingling in fingertips and toes, not interfering with activities of daily living). Also noted: Grade 1 alopecia (total scalp alopecia by cycle 2, consistent with paclitaxel). Grade 1 nausea (well-controlled with antiemetics). No dose reductions required at this time. Neuropathy monitored at each cycle. No neutropenic fever. ANC nadir cycle 2: 1,200 cells/uL (Grade 1 neutropenia). Hemoglobin: 9.8 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, no transfusion required). Platelets: 148,000/uL (within normal limits).

At age 52, after cycle 3 of carboplatin/paclitaxel (mid-treatment restaging), the patient had a CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast, which showed cancer responding to therapy. Findings: No recurrence of ascites. No residual omental mass. No peritoneal implants identified above the limits of CT resolution. No lymphadenopathy in pelvic or para-aortic regions (previously enlarged nodes now within normal limits). Liver: no surface or parenchymal lesions. Diaphragm: no nodular thickening. Post-surgical changes noted in pelvis. Impression: No radiographically evident disease. Response consistent with complete radiographic response to date. CA-125 at time of imaging: 78 U/mL (trending down from 342 U/mL post-surgery). Continued response to platinum-based chemotherapy.

At age 52, mid-treatment clinical note after cycle 3. Patient tolerating chemotherapy reasonably well. Reports persistent Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (bilateral feet more than hands), which has been stable and non-progressive. Paclitaxel dose reduction considered but deferred given patient preference to maintain dose density. Fatigue CTCAE Grade 2 (limiting instrumental ADLs). No neutropenic fever episodes. No hospitalizations. CA-125 trending down: 78 U/mL (normal <35 U/mL approaching). Plan: continue carboplatin/paclitaxel cycles 4-6. Reinforce neuropathy monitoring. Discuss PARP inhibitor maintenance planning. Cancer present but responding.

At age 52 years, the patient received cycles 4, 5, and 6 of carboplatin AUC 6 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days (completing 6-cycle course). Cycle 4: administered at full dose, Grade 1 neutropenia (ANC nadir 1,050) managed with dose held 1 week then administered. Cycle 5: paclitaxel dose reduced to 135 mg/m2 (dose level -1) due to worsening Grade 2-3 peripheral neuropathy (Grade 3 neuropathy with difficulty buttoning clothing and foot drop on left side). Carboplatin maintained at AUC 6. Cycle 6: paclitaxel 135 mg/m2, carboplatin AUC 6, administered per schedule. 6-cycle platinum/taxane chemotherapy course completed. End-of-treatment CA-125: 18 U/mL (within normal range, normalization achieved).

At age 52, after completion of 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel, Grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy documented (difficulty with fine motor tasks, left foot drop requiring physical therapy referral). Neuropathy attributed to cumulative paclitaxel toxicity. Physical therapy initiated. Also noted: complete alopecia (Grade 2, resolving 6-8 weeks post-chemotherapy). Grade 1 fatigue persisting. No long-term renal toxicity from carboplatin (creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, stable). No ototoxicity. Patient referred for neurology consultation for foot drop evaluation - EMG/nerve conduction studies ordered.

At age 52, post-completion of 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel (end-of-treatment restaging CT), the patient had a CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast and PET-CT, which showed no cancer (complete radiographic response). CT findings: No ascites. No peritoneal implants identified. No lymphadenopathy. No pleural effusion. No liver lesions. Post-surgical pelvic changes. PET-CT: No FDG-avid disease identified anywhere. No abnormal metabolic activity in the pelvis, abdomen, chest, or elsewhere. SUVmax of residual post-surgical tissue in pelvis: 1.4 (background). Impression: Complete metabolic and radiographic response to primary debulking surgery and 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel. No evidence of disease. CA-125: 18 U/mL (normal). Suitable candidate for maintenance PARP inhibitor therapy.

At age 52 (approximately 9 months after initial diagnosis), end-of-chemotherapy assessment. Complete clinical, radiographic, and biochemical response confirmed. CA-125 18 U/mL (normal). PET-CT negative. ECOG performance status 1 (limited by residual neuropathy). Neuropathy improving on physical therapy - foot drop resolving. Discussed maintenance therapy options: given germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC), HRD-positive tumor, and complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy, patient is an excellent candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance. Options reviewed: niraparib (FDA approved for first-line maintenance regardless of BRCA status, with enhanced benefit in BRCA-mutant/HRD-positive), olaparib (FDA approved for BRCA-mutant HRD-positive ovarian cancer, first-line maintenance). Discussed landmark PRIMA and SOLO-1 trial data. Patient elected niraparib based on once-daily dosing convenience. Individualized starting dose discussed - patient weight 72 kg, platelets 189,000/uL - per PRIMA trial protocol individualized starting dose (ISD): niraparib 200 mg once daily (weight <77 kg or platelets <150,000 threshold - patient is borderline, oncologist elected 200 mg ISD to mitigate hematologic toxicity risk). Cancer absent. Complete response.

At age 52 years (approximately 9.5 months after initial diagnosis), the patient was initiated on niraparib (PARP inhibitor) maintenance therapy at individualized starting dose of 200 mg orally once daily (standard 300 mg dose reduced per individualized dosing protocol due to weight 72 kg and clinical judgment regarding hematologic risk). Indication: first-line maintenance for FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, following complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy. Labs at start: Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, Platelets 189,000/uL, ANC 2,400/uL, Creatinine 0.84 mg/dL. Patient counseled on monitoring for hematologic toxicity (thrombocytopenia, anemia, neutropenia), fatigue, nausea, and rare risk of MDS/AML. CBC ordered weekly for first month, then monthly.

At age 52, approximately 3 weeks after initiating niraparib 200 mg daily, the patient developed Grade 3 thrombocytopenia. CBC results: Platelets 42,000/uL (baseline 189,000/uL at start of niraparib). Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia). ANC 1,800/uL (within normal limits). Patient asymptomatic - no petechiae, bruising, or bleeding. Niraparib held per package insert guidelines (hold for platelets <100,000/uL until recovery to >100,000/uL). Patient instructed to avoid NSAIDs, aspirin, anticoagulants. Repeat CBC in 1 week. No transfusion required. Oncology nurse navigator notified. Patient seen urgently in clinic for CBC review and examination.

At age 52, urgent clinic visit for niraparib-related Grade 3 thrombocytopenia. Platelets nadir 42,000/uL at week 3 of niraparib. Physical exam: no petechiae, no ecchymoses, no bleeding. ECOG PS 1. Niraparib held. Bone marrow suppression pattern consistent with niraparib-related hematologic toxicity (expected on-target PARP inhibition effect in bone marrow progenitor cells). Hematology consulted to rule out immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) and other etiologies - peripheral smear reviewed: no schistocytes, no platelet clumping, thrombocytopenia consistent with drug-related myelosuppression. Bone marrow biopsy deferred (no evidence of MDS morphology on peripheral smear, no dysplastic features). Weekly CBCs ordered. Plan: resume niraparib at reduced dose (150 mg daily) once platelets recover to >100,000/uL. Reinforce avoidance of platelet-inhibiting medications. Cancer absent.

At age 52, follow-up CBC at 2 weeks after niraparib hold: Platelets recovered to 118,000/uL (recovery from Grade 3 thrombocytopenia nadir of 42,000/uL). Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL (stable, Grade 1 anemia). ANC 2,200/uL (normal). Niraparib restarted at dose-reduced level of 150 mg orally once daily per protocol (one dose level reduction from 200 mg starting dose). Patient counseled on continued CBC monitoring. CBC to be checked weekly for 4 weeks, then monthly if stable. No recurrence of severe thrombocytopenia at reduced dose anticipated but close monitoring essential.

At age 52 years (approximately 11 months after initial diagnosis), niraparib resumed at reduced dose of 150 mg orally once daily after platelet recovery. Patient tolerating dose-reduced niraparib. CBC monitoring shows platelet stabilization at 105,000-135,000/uL range (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia, <150,000/uL but no further Grade 2 or higher events). Hemoglobin stabilized at 10.5-11.2 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, no transfusion required). Niraparib 150 mg daily continued as long-term maintenance. Planned duration: until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. CA-125 monitoring every 3 months.

At age 53 (approximately 15 months after initial diagnosis, 6 months after starting niraparib maintenance), the patient had a CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (surveillance imaging), which showed no cancer. Findings: No ascites. No peritoneal implants or carcinomatosis. No pelvic or para-aortic lymphadenopathy. No suspicious lesions in liver or elsewhere in abdomen. Post-surgical changes in pelvis unchanged from prior. No new findings. Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. CA-125: 14 U/mL (normal, stable). Continued complete remission on niraparib maintenance.

At age 53 (15 months post-diagnosis), routine 3-month surveillance visit on niraparib 150 mg daily. Patient doing well overall. Neuropathy from paclitaxel substantially improved (residual Grade 1 - mild numbness in toes bilaterally, no functional limitation). Foot drop resolved. Hair regrowth complete. Fatigue Grade 1 (minimal, not limiting activities). Tolerating niraparib 150 mg well at this dose level. Platelets stable at 112,000/uL (Grade 1, asymptomatic). CBC trend stable - no further dose reductions required. CA-125: 14 U/mL (normal). CT negative for disease. ECOG PS 0-1. Menopausal symptoms: hot flashes and vaginal dryness managed with non-hormonal agents (venlafaxine 37.5 mg daily for vasomotor symptoms; vaginal lubricants). Discussed BRCA1 implications - patient's daughter (age 24) has undergone genetic counseling and BRCA1 testing - test result positive (confirmed 5382insC variant). Referral to high-risk program for daughter placed. Cancer absent. In complete remission.

At age 53 (21 months after initial diagnosis, 12 months of niraparib maintenance), the patient had a CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (annual surveillance), which showed no cancer. Findings: Stable post-operative changes in pelvis. No ascites. No peritoneal nodules or masses. No lymphadenopathy. Liver, spleen, kidneys normal. No adnexal masses (bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy). Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. CA-125: 12 U/mL (normal, trending stable/slightly down). Patient remains in complete radiographic and biochemical remission on niraparib maintenance therapy.

At age 53 (21 months post-diagnosis, 12 months of niraparib maintenance), annual surveillance visit. Patient in complete remission. CA-125 12 U/mL. CT negative. Niraparib 150 mg daily well-tolerated at this dose. Thrombocytopenia stable at Grade 1 (platelets 108,000-125,000/uL, no new Grade 2+ events since dose reduction). Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL (stable Grade 1 anemia, erythropoiesis-stimulating agent not initiated given stability). No cytopenias requiring further dose reduction. Quality of life discussion: patient reports good functional status (returned to full-time work as librarian), ECOG 0. Peripheral neuropathy Grade 1 residual, not progressing. Cognitive function intact. Psychosocial: mild anxiety related to cancer recurrence fear, referred to oncology social work/support group for ovarian cancer survivors. BRCA1-related counseling: discussed risk-reducing mastectomy option per NCCN guidelines for BRCA1 carriers; patient is post-bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (already accomplished as part of cytoreductive surgery); breast surveillance with annual mammography and MRI recommended; patient up-to-date with breast MRI (negative). Cancer absent. Complete remission. Continue niraparib maintenance.

At age 54 (approximately 27 months post-diagnosis, 18 months of niraparib maintenance), routine surveillance visit. Patient continues in complete remission. CA-125: 11 U/mL (normal, stable). No new symptoms. Niraparib 150 mg daily continued without further modification. CBC: Platelets 119,000/uL (stable Grade 1), Hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL (stable Grade 1 anemia), ANC 2,100/uL (normal). No evidence of MDS on routine CBC differential - no dysplastic changes. Quality of life: excellent, ECOG 0. Peripheral neuropathy: Grade 1 residual, essentially resolved (patient reports only very mild tingling in toes with prolonged standing, not bothersome). Patient enrolled in BRCA1 ovarian cancer survivor registry study. Breast surveillance: breast MRI performed 2 months prior, negative. Discussed option of prophylactic mastectomy again - patient deferred, prefers surveillance approach. Next surveillance imaging in 6 months. Cancer absent. Durable complete remission on PARP inhibitor maintenance.

scenario_blurb: High-grade serous ovarian cancer FIGO stage IIIC in a 52-year-old with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant. Presents with ascites and peritoneal carcinomatosis. Undergoes primary debulking surgery achieving R0 resection, followed by 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel with CA-125 normalization. Started on niraparib maintenance therapy. Develops thrombocytopenia requiring dose reduction.
